title: Managing Workloads — Chaining and filtering
text:
Because
kubectl
outputs resource names in the same syntax it accepts, you can chain operations
using
$()
or
xargs
:
kubectl get
$(
kubectl create -f docs/concepts/cluster-administration/nginx/ -o name
|
grep service/
)
kubectl create -f docs/concepts/cluster-administration/nginx/ -o name
|
grep service/
|
xargs -i kubectl get
'{}'
The output might be similar to:
NAME           TYPE           CLUSTER-IP   EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)      AGE
my-nginx-svc   LoadBalancer   10.0.0.208   <pending>     80/TCP       0s
With the above commands, first you create resources under
docs/concepts/cluster-administration/nginx/
and print
the resources created with
-o name
output format (print each resource as resource/name).
Then you
grep
only the Service, and then print it with
kubectl get
.
